fuzzy_prd:
We're building the operational transformation core for a real-time collaborative document editing backend. Multiple clients can edit the same document at the same time and we need a library that keeps everything consistent. The library needs to handle the full lifecycle of a collaborative document: checking whether an incoming change is even valid, looking up which editing strategy a document uses by either its short name or its full identifier, applying changes to a versioned document snapshot, and transforming a queued-up change so it still makes sense after someone else's change was already applied. Documents can be created from scratch, have edits applied to their content, or be fully deleted — each of these needs to behave correctly and return precise status information. When something goes wrong, we need stable, named error categories rather than free-form messages, because clients rely on these to branch their error-handling logic. We also need to handle cursor-like presence markers: when a change is applied to the document, any collaborator's cursor position should shift correctly — similar to how the version-advance logic works for the main document. Finally, we need a batch-apply path that processes a sequence of changes one after another, stopping cleanly at the first problem. The whole system should be built so the core rules are completely separate from how commands come in over the wire.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: on success, apply prints exactly three lines in this order: 'v=<version>', 'type=<canonical identifier, short name, or null>', 'data=<canonical JSON with sorted object keys, or the literal token absent when no content>'. All lines end with a newline. If there’s missing content, that should come out as the word 'absent', not as 'null'.

Also, for deletes, the del field must be the literal boolean true. Anything else, including truthy values like 1 or the string 'true', means the envelope is badly formed and checkOp returns ERR_OT_OP_BADLY_FORMED.

One more transform edge case to lock down: when both the pending op and the applied op declare a version but they differ, transform returns ERR_OP_VERSION_MISMATCH_DURING_TRANSFORM. And for apply behavior, if we’re trying to apply an edit (op payload) to a snapshot that has no type, that returns ERR_DOC_DOES_NOT_EXIST.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: exports.checkOp = function(op); exports.normalizeType = function(typeName); exports.apply = function(snapshot, op); exports.transform = function(type, op, appliedOp); exports.transformPresence = function(presence, op, isOwnOp); exports.applyOps = function(snapshot, ops)

trigger_keywords: what is the canonical URI for the json0 type
what does normalizeType return for json0
what is the full identifier of the JSON OT type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The canonical URI for json0 is exactly 'http://sharejs.org/types/JSONv0'. normalizeType('json0') returns this string.

trigger_keywords: what error when a type does not support presence
what category when transformPresence is called on json0
what if the presence marker type has no transformPresence method
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the document type does not support presence (lacks a transformPresence method), transformPresence returns ERR_TYPE_DOES_NOT_SUPPORT_PRESENCE. For example, json0 does not support presence.

trigger_keywords: what is the empty/default content for a new json0 document
what data does a create with no initial data produce for json0
what value does the json0 type use as its initial content
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a json0 document is created without supplying initial data, its content is null (rendered as 'data=null' in the output, not 'data=absent').

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how the version-advance logic works for the main document
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The presence marker's version field v is incremented by one after each transformPresence call, mirroring the snapshot version increment in exports.apply

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that detail. Please refer to the start.md specification and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for authoritative requirements.

feature1_check_op.json.description: Validate the structural well-formedness of a single operation envelope. A well-formed envelope is a non-null map that declares exactly one intent: a create whose payload is a map naming a registered document type, a deletion flag set to the literal boolean true, or an edit payload under the op field. Optional source and sequence identifiers, when present, must appear together with the correct primitive shapes (source a string, sequence a number). The check reports whether the envelope is well-formed, and on failure reports a normalized category distinguishing a malformed envelope from a reference to an unregistered type.

feature2_normalize_type.json.description: Resolve a document-type reference, given either as a short type name or as its canonical identifier, to that type's canonical identifier string. A reference that names no registered type resolves to nothing.

feature3_1_apply_version_guard.json.description: Guard applied before mutating a snapshot: when both the snapshot and the operation carry a version they must be equal, otherwise application is rejected with a normalized version-mismatch category. When either side omits its version the guard is skipped and the operation is applied normally, updating the document and advancing its version.

feature3_2_apply_create.json.description: Applying a create operation to an empty snapshot initializes the document: it adopts the named type's canonical identifier, sets its content to the supplied initial data (or the type's empty value when none is supplied), and advances the version by one. Creating over a snapshot that already has a type is rejected with a normalized already-exists category and leaves the snapshot untouched.

feature3_3_apply_delete.json.description: Applying a delete operation clears the document: its content becomes absent and its type becomes null, and the version advances by one. This holds whether or not the document currently exists.

feature3_4_apply_edit.json.description: Applying an edit operation hands its payload to the document's type to transform the current content, advancing the version by one on success. Editing a document that does not exist is rejected with a normalized does-not-exist category; editing when the snapshot references an unregistered type is rejected with a normalized type-not-recognized category.

feature3_5_apply_noop.json.description: An operation that carries neither create, delete, nor edit intent leaves the document's content and type unchanged but still advances the version by one. This holds both for an existing document and for a snapshot that has no content yet.

feature4_transform.json.description: Transform a pending operation so it can be applied after another operation that was already applied at the same base version, mutating the pending operation in place and advancing its version when it carries one.

feature5_transform_presence.json.description: Transform a presence marker so it stays consistent after an operation is applied.

feature6_apply_ops.json.description: Apply an ordered sequence of operations to a snapshot.
